data.title: Uber: The 'Netflix Of Mobility' Set To Gain Big From The Autonomous Vehicle Race
data.description:
Despite reaching recent market highs, Uber Technologies (NYSE:UBER) is a compelling long-term investment opportunity as a profitable, cash-generating, global blue-chip platform that will benefit from secular trends in transportation and online delivery.

Its entrenched and leadership market position in transport and food-delivery enables Uber to leverage its scale and improve its already profitable asset-light business model. Different global partnerships with autonomous vehicle companies (AV), both in the US and internationally, make it a standout in the mobility space. As (Netflix) or (Amazon) have dominated the streaming and e-commerce business, similar reasons are also driving Uber's success.

For years, investors have feared that regulatory risks tied to the gig economy could undermine Uber’s business model, especially as courts and lawmakers increasingly push to classify drivers as employees rather than independent contractors. The rise of AVs could render this debate obsolete. More importantly, AVs should improve unit economics and expand the addressable market for ride-hailing, as car ownership declines and consumers increasingly turn to shared mobility. In that context, Uber stands to benefit from higher efficiency, lower costs, and broader adoption.

While Uber trades at a premium compared to peers (around 20% if you see EV/EBITDA), the market is pricing in over 30% revenue growth an´d a substantial improvement in EBITDA margin, from the current 10% to 30%. While these outcomes are possible, they appear to some level priced in at current levels. I recommend considering entry below $90-100 or selling puts to generate income and potentially acquire shares at a discount. For patient investors, I wouldn't mind buying at these levels given the long-term upside.

Global Brand with Network Effects and Duopoly Dynamics

Uber has become a verb in a market with limited differentiation. Its ride-hailing business operates under one of the most recognized and trusted transportation brands globally, with a presence in over 70 countries, where it leads in most of them. In the US, Uber and Lyft effectively function as a duopoly in the ride-sharing space. In other major markets for Uber, as the UK, Mexico, Canada, Brazil, and Australia, Uber holds a dominant position, often capturing over 60–70% share.

While the company once faced intense competition, the industry has seen significant consolidation. Uber exited markets with strong local rivals, such as China, India, and several Southeast Asian countries, by selling its operations to competitors like Grab. In the remaining markets, Uber has solidified its leadership position.

This scale-driven dominance provides durable pricing power and reduces customer churn. Moreover, Uber benefits from increased asset utilization (very relevant for AI players) and operating leverage as its network grows. Regarding the delivery business, although Uber is the second-largest player in the U.S., it still benefits from significant network effects and the Uber One loyalty program. While profitability remains a challenge for grocery delivery, speed and efficiency are increasingly important. This is why companies like Instacart have outperformed players like FreshDirect. Uber’s extensive network positions it well to compete and potentially win in this space.

AV: Business Model and Profitability Boost

I envision Uber becoming the Netflix of transportation, the leading platform that aggregates and distributes services, while enabling other players in the ecosystem to operate through it. In the same way, Netflix emerged as the dominant force in the streaming industry by controlling the customer interface while working with studios, content creators, producers, and actors, Uber is positioning itself similarly in mobility and delivery.

While Uber and Lyft operate in the referred duopoly in the US, the AV space is far more fragmented, with numerous players such as Waymo, (MBLY), May Mobility, and various Chinese companies all vying for their space.

We’ve seen similar dynamics in other industries. In retail logistics, asset-light platforms like (CART), (DOOR), and (AMZN) have outperformed asset-heavy models like Ocado or FreshDirect. Ultimately, what matters most is control over the customer. Achieving this requires significant investment in network effects, something Uber has already done at scale. Recently, smaller competitors like InDrive have attempted unsuccessfully to enter US market.

As illustrated in the graph below provided by Lyft, the Robotaxi value-chain is broad and involves very different activities and tasks. Balancing demand, managing fleet, etc, are different businesses from constructing AV. Uber, through its platform, holds a powerful position between self-driving technology and end consumers.

Very important to that, is how AV is a changing catalyst for Uber’s unit economics is the deployment of AVs. Driver compensation accounts for around 75% of an Uber ride. By removing the human driver, gross margins should expand substantially, improving profitability per ride. Legal and regulatory risks will decline as AVs reduce labor classification disputes. Efficiency and asset utilization will increase, especially with 24/7 robotaxi availability. AV providers compete to be on Uber’s platform, ensuring Uber maintains pricing power and consumer access, without the need for full vertical integration.

The fact that Uber is having partnerships with most AV companies is a proof of that. Also, for robotaxis to recover investments, full utilization of AV cars is necessary and probably only Uber is capable of doing that in most markets. For 2025 and 2026, aside from the launches in many US cities, Uber has mentioned it will be deploying robotaxis in Tokyo, Abu Dhabi, etc.

Finally, as has been noted repeatedly, it will likely take many years for autonomous vehicles (AVs) to become mainstream in the U.S. The technology still faces significant challenges, particularly in adverse weather conditions like rain and snow.In the meantime, ride-sharing platforms will continue to grow more rapidly, benefiting from greater operating leverage as adoption increases. In the US, regulatory progress remains slow and fragmented, often handled at the state level, which adds to the timeline, as even Elon Musk has pointed out in his public frustrations.

And while international expansion may seem appealing, keep in mind that dense traffic and infrastructure challenges abroad may further delay AV adoption in many global cities.

From Growth to Durable Profitability

Uber has successfully transitioned from a cash-burning disruptor to a mature, blue-chip technology platform. The company is now consistently profitable on a GAAP basis, generating positive free cash flow, and has demonstrated strong cost discipline and capital efficiency. This shift has validated its platform model and de-risked the long-term investment case. It is also in the moment where it starts to gain cash from leverage, with operating income and EBITDA growing over 100% YoY. Other revenue streams as advertisements, should help improve unit economics in the same way they helped Inscart become profitable.

On a financial perspective, with nearly half of its revenue generated outside the U.S., Uber stands to benefit from a weaker U.S. dollar, which will inflate the dollar-reported value of international revenue and cash flow.

Finally, there are promising opportunities in emerging segments such as Uber One, loyalty programs, online groceries, alcohol, and pharmacy deliveries. While Uber may have some regrets about the Cornershop acquisition due to integration challenges, the market still holds significant long-term potential. Finally, AVs could play a key role in making the delivery business more profitable. Compared to ride-sharing, the delivery business has always faced tougher unit economics.

Valuation

Uber trades at a premium compared to its peers, particularly Lyft, which, in my view, represents a high-conviction investment and a once-in-a-lifetime opportunity. However, I believe the premium is justified considering its global presence, dominant position in most markets, and strong brand

Also, Uber is executing a $4 Bn share buy-back which should improve the P/E and EV / EBITDA as the number of shares is reduced. This programe represents around 4% of Uber´s market capitalization which should also provide some support.

Based on the table below, the EV/EBITDA multiple of 22x appears quite stretched. This valuation implies an expected growth rate of over 50%. While aggressive, such growth is possible, as operating leverage takes effect. The current 22X multiple also assumes EBITDA margins will rise to around 30%, which may seem ambitious but could be attainable under leverage, AV, the right execution.

I run a DCF considering $80 Bn of revenues by 2029, a 9% WACC, 4% of terminal growth, and an EBITDA margin expansion, from the current 10-12% to 30%, driven by operating leverage.

The FCF terminal value considered is the last three years average to make our forecasts more conservative. As you can see, the model yields a valuation above $100 per share.

Risks

I believe the main risk is that Uber's stock has already seen significant growth, making the current valuation somewhat stretched. For patient investors, I wouldn't mind holding or even buying at current levels, but some caution is warranted.

That said, despite my earlier comments on autonomous vehicles (AVs), there's still a competitive threat, particularly from Waymo, which benefits from the deep financial backing of Alphabet. The latter could decide to burn cash for the very long term and hinder Uber´s position.

Final remarks

Uber has evolved into a tech-enabled transportation utility, globally integrated, capital-light, and increasingly automated. Its ongoing profitability improvement, combined with potential upside from AV, presents a strong case for margin expansion and sustained long-term earnings growth.

With its market dominance and widespread app adoption, Uber is well-positioned to expand (and further) into adjacent verticals such as fintech (e.g., driver lending), advertising, and other high-margin opportunities.

Given these dynamics, Uber stands out as a compelling long-term core holding for a forward-looking equity portfolio. Over the next 5 to 10 years, I believe it has the potential to become a trillion-dollar market capitalization company in a decade, which is multiplying by 5 its current $200 Bn value.

data.title: Spotify Technology Is A Winner, But Not A Buy At This High Price
data.description:
Why I’m Hitting Pause on Spotify’s Stock

Here’s where I’m at with Spotify Technology (NYSE: NYSE:SPOT): they’ve made the switch from a high-growth cash burner to a business that actually makes money—and that’s no small feat. Profitability isn’t just a headline; it’s starting to show up consistently and that alone is a huge deal if you’ve followed their story for a while. The catch is, the market has caught on. Over the past year, the stock has exploded, more than doubling and it’s up over 40% just since the last time I looked at it. At $705 a share, I think most of the easy upside has been made.

If I had to sum up my stance, I’m saying Hold—not because I’m suddenly negative but because the trade-off isn’t as good as before. All the good news is out in the open now. To move higher from here, Spotify will need to hit nearly every target they set, or make a big difference with a surprise. There’s not much room for mistakes when expectations are this high.

How Spotify Keeps Leading the Pack

Spotify is still the king of streaming music and audio, for sure. With 678 million users around the world, they’re the platform everyone thinks of when they hear “streaming music.” What stands out lately is how they’ve gone beyond music—bringing in podcasts and more recently, audiobooks. They’re not just talking about being a “platform”; they’re making it happen.

The company’s financial results support this story. This past quarter, Spotify pulled in €4.19 billion in revenue, which is up 15% from last year. They managed to control their spending on research and development and sales and marketing, even while growing. Net income landed at €225 million and gross profit was the highest it’s ever been. That’s not just luck; that’s just good management.

Let’s not forget about the business moves—they’re always looking for new ways to grow. Rumors of a Netflix tie-up are just the latest example of how they’re thinking about the future, not just the next quarter.

The Streaming World: More Crowded Than Ever

Audio streaming as a whole is still growing but there’s a lot more competition than there used to be. Podcasts are a must-have and AI-powered playlists and personalized content are quickly becoming the standard. Spotify is still ahead when it comes to users and time spent on the platform but Apple Music, Amazon, YouTube and even smaller companies are not sitting still.

Regulation is another big topic, especially in Europe where new rules about how apps can charge users could change how much money Spotify gets to keep from every subscription. That might help them, or it might just make things more complicated. Either way, regulatory shifts are something to watch.

What the Numbers Really Say

The story is in the numbers. In the latest quarter, Spotify’s revenue hit €4.19 billion, up 15%. Gross profit reached €1.33 billion, with a margin above 31%, which is better than it’s ever been. Operating income was €509 million, miles ahead of last year. Free cash flow remains strong and they’re sitting on €7 billion in cash—plenty of money for whatever comes next. The company has relatively little debt, a healthy return on equity and they’re now funding their own growth instead of needing investors to give them more money.

One thing that stands out is that margins are finally looking solid and the days of “profit someday” are gone. Spotify is delivering real earnings, not just to grow.

Is Spotify’s Price Too High?

Now, about the price. This is where things get harder. Spotify trades at a forward P/E ratio of about 72, with EV/EBITDA over 70 and price-to-book around 22. Even compared to other high-growth tech companies, that’s a steep valuation. You have to believe that the company will keep growing at double digits and keep finding ways to improve margins for the next several years to justify paying this much.

If you run the math on what analysts expect—almost $19 in earnings per share by 2027—and even use a generous multiple, the fair value you come up with is pretty much right where the stock trades today. There’s just not a big gap between where Spotify is and where the numbers say it could go, unless something changes.

Compared to peers, the stock looks expensive on almost every measure. It’s more expensive than Netflix on an earnings basis and it trades at a big premium to traditional media and digital companies. You can make the case that it deserves some of that premium but at this level, it seems too high.

What the Headlines and Analysts Are Saying

The talk about Spotify has been mostly positive. Analyst sentiment still says they are doing well in streaming audio and there’s a lot of excitement about what they might do with partnerships or new technology. Headlines show plenty of optimism but it’s clear that expectations are higher than ever and the market is watching closely to see if Spotify can keep delivering real progress—not just big promises.

Where Things Could Go Wrong

Here’s where I’m keeping my guard up. The main risk is simple: valuation. At these prices, if they fall short or slow down—on revenue, profit, or even guidance—it could send the stock much lower. Competitors like Apple and Amazon can afford to compete on price for as long as they want, which could force Spotify to spend more or cut prices. The cost of music rights isn’t going down either and negotiations with big record labels are always hard. If ad spending takes a hit in a weak economy, Spotify’s ad-supported business will feel the pain. Regulatory issues are also there, especially in Europe. Even with the best management team and execution, things can change for the worse.

On the bright side, the company’s move into podcasts and audiobooks, its strong brand and the fact that it’s sitting on so much cash help protect against these risks. They have options.

What to Watch Next

What happens next? The next couple of quarters are going to be important. Investors are watching to see if Spotify can keep growing margins and getting more money from each user. Over the long haul, the expectation is still for steady, strong growth and higher profits over time, as more people pay for premium and the company finds new ways to monetize content.

If Spotify can deliver a big surprise—a new business, a partnership, or a new technology that stands out—there could be more upside. But after a 136% rally in a year, I think it’s fair to say expectations are very high.

How the Future Could Play Out

In the worst case, a global recession, a price war, or a failed negotiation with record labels could cause revenue growth to slow and margins to shrink, sending the stock back toward $400–$500. In the “base case,” Spotify hits consensus: steady growth, a bit more margin expansion and decent cash flow. The stock probably trades sideways or creeps a little higher, maybe into the $650–$725 range. The bull case would take a big win: big price hikes that stick, a major leap forward in podcasts or audiobooks, or a killer new product that catches fire. Then and only then, do I see the stock making a run at $900 or more—but to be honest, that scenario doesn’t feel far from what’s already included in the current price.

My Final Take

To wrap this up, I still believe Spotify is a fantastic company and an industry leader. But at $705 a share, with all the great news and big expectations already priced in, I don’t see the same opportunity I saw before. If you already own it, I’d hang on and see how the next few quarters turn out. If you’re thinking about buying, I’d wait for a pullback—something in the $600–$625 range would get my attention, since that would bring Spotify’s valuation down to a more reasonable level and offer a better margin of safety if growth slows or the market stumbles, especially if the setback isn't tied to fundamentals. At this stage, patience pays. Let Spotify catch its breath and see if another leg higher is really in the cards.

data.title: Evidence That The Banking System Is In Strong Shape
data.description:
Not too much has been said about the banking industry these days.

The banking system is, of course, a very important foundation for the performance of the economy, and if the banking system is doing well...it can be assumed that the economic system, as a whole, is doing pretty well.

Right now, I would argue that the banking system is doing just fine, and that this would indicate that the foundation for the economy is in pretty good shape.

Telis Demos writes in the Wall Street Journal that in this uncertain world.

"The biggest U.S. banks at least stand to profit from investors’ anxiety, via their Wall Street trading desks."

"These operations have kicked into high gear, providing a gusher of revenue and profit for big banks that reported second quarter results this week, including JPMorgan Chase and Goldman Sachs. And a closer look suggests this could continue into future quarters, too."

"Markets are helping out trading desks by giving nervy clients plenty of reasons to trade. And many banks are allocating more of their capital to trading right now."

But, this is not all.

In another Wall Street Journal article, Mr. Demos writes about smaller banks, banks that don't deal in all the trading activity.

Mr. Demos writes about the fact that commercial and industrial loan activity, as reported by the Federal Reserve System, has been experiencing a pickup, especially in the second quarter of 2025.

The increase, year-over-year, is only about 3 percent presently, but these loans seem to be increasing going into the summer months.

"Many bankers have...attributed the uptick to the uncertainty brought about by tariffs: Companies drew down existing lines of credit. Often, this wasn’t to fund new capital expenditures, but to load up on inventory. In other words, companies were playing defense, not offense."z

The point is, however, that the movement is up.

And, there is one more point I would like to make that, I believe, is very important in the current situation.

The commercial banks have the "cash" to do these things.

In fact, they have an unusually large amount of "cash" on hand to do these things.

I have been writing about this factor for a relatively long time now.

Just look at the "cash" commercial banks have on hand, currently, compared with what they held in the past.

Wow!

For most of the decade of the 2010s, commercial banks held less than $400 billion in cash assets. Currently, these banks are holding almost $3.6 trillion in cash.

The point is, the banking system has the "cash on hand" to do all the things mentioned above without the Federal Reserve doing anything to provide the commercial banking system the resources it might need to stimulate the system.

The commercial banking system is very, very liquid.

The commercial banking system can do what it wants. The bigger banks can go about their trading business, taking advantage of the "uncertainty" created by the Trump tariff battle.

The smaller banks can become more active in their lending business as the demand for loans rises.

And, this is the really important part of the signals coming from the commercial banking system.

If the lending at smaller banks rises and continues to rise, this would be an indicator that the economy…as a whole…was becoming stronger.

This is the kind of signal that investors should be looking for.

But, also keep an eye on what commercial banks are doing with their "cash."

At its peak, the "cash assets" of commercial banks almost reached $4.4 trillion. These "cash assets" have only dropped to a little below $3.6 trillion.

Note that this level has been reached after more than three years of the Fed's quantitative tightening.

The Federal Reserve is still conducting a policy of quantitative tightening, but its impact appears to be very minimal now.

This means that the smaller commercial banks have lots and lots of "cash" that they could release into the economic system and add a lot of pressure for a future rise in prices.

Conclusion

The commercial banking system is doing well and has lots of money on hand to help inflate the economic system going forward.

This is good news about the economy.

It is not so good news if the large amounts of "excess reserves" in the banking system turn into excessive increases in prices.

This could be, however, exactly what President Trump is looking for.

data.title: Schwab Earnings Preview: Cheap On A PEG Basis, Can The Stock Break Out Of This 3-Year Consolidation?
data.description:
Schwab (NYSE:SCHW) will report their calendar Q2 ’25 financial results on Friday morning, July 18th.

Analyst consensus is expecting $1.10 in earnings per share on $5.73 billion in revenue for expected y-o-y growth of 51% and 22%. Pre-tax income is expected at $2.77 billion for expected y-o-y growth of 44%.

Since Schwab peaked at $96 per share in early February ’22, the stock originally fell to the $50 area (and even a little lower than that) in 2023 (twice) but has slowly made its way back to within 9-iron distance of its former all-time high in the last two years.

Schwab is up 24% YTD (as of Wednesday night’s 7/16 close).

When SCHW peaked in early '22, it was trading at 40x earnings, for what was expected to be 20% y-o-y EPS growth, while today SCHW is trading at 20x for an expected EPS growth rate in ’25 of 37% on revenue growth of 15%.

A high-multiple momentum stock is now a reasonably-valued PEG (pe-to-growth) play.

SCHW EPS and revenue estimate revisions:

SCHW’s 2025 EPS estimated bottomed in late ’24 and has been revised steadily higher since that time.

Schwab’s revenue estimate revisions have followed the same pattern.

In Q1 ’25, revenue grew 18%, pre-tax income was up 33%, and EPS rose 41%. Net new assets alone grew $39 billion in March ’25. Jefferies made an interesting point in their post-earnings analysis that TD Ameritrade’s business is slowly returning to Schwab, accounting for 2%–3% organic growth in Q1 ’25. Jefferies also noted that Schwab currently has roughly 30% wallet share of TDAM’s clients that were onboarded, while that should be closer to 50%.

Summary/conclusion:

With Schwab’s merger/acquisition of TD Ameritrade now behind it, Schwab’s investment thesis returns to a “fundamental” one. Trading at 20x forward EPS for expected earnings growth in ’25, ’26, and ’27 of 37%, 18%, and 16%, on expected revenue growth for the same three years of 15%, 12%, and 8%, the valuation story is self-explanatory.

Technically, a break and heavy volume close above $96–$97, after a three-year consolidation period, would be a very favorable technical sign.

Schwab recently raised the quarterly dividend from $0.25 to $0.27 per share, the first increase in 8 quarters.

As an advisor who has all clients' assets with Charles Schwab as a 3rd-party custodian, I was relieved Schwab didn’t chase the crypto or bitcoin craze in the 2020’s, but watching the rise of Robinhood of late, and in particular after Amazon (AMZN, AMZN:CA) and Walmart (WMT, WMT:CA) announced they were considering converting to stablecoin (US dollar-backed crypto) to reduce the cost of the interchange fees from credit cards, I wondered if this wasn’t the ultimate legitimization of crypto, by America’s two largest retailers, and if Schwab is now behind the “Bitcoin” boat.

As the low-cost leader in the former “discount-broker” realm, Schwab is still an asset-gathering juggernaut. The net new assets that get brought in every quarter is still quite formidable.

With current growth expectations of 37% in EPS and 15% revenue growth in calendar ’25 and the current multiple 20x, the stock is way too cheap here. If a $125 fair value price is used for Schwab, the stock is still just trading at 28x for 37% expected EPS growth this year.

Schwab is this blog’s 2nd largest financial weighting behind JPM (JPM) and in the top 10 of client holdings. Part articles on Schwab are here, and here.

None of this is advice or a recommendation, but only an opinion. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. None of the information above may be updated, and if updated may not be done so in a timely fashion.

Thanks for reading.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Freshpet: Stock Plunge And Growth Make For A Dilemma
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I grant Freshpet, Inc. (NASDAQ:FRPT) a Hold rating. I think that it is advisable to be prudent and wait until the company becomes a profitable firm. Since 2016, the company has only achieved positive net earnings in 2024. As such, it has financed its operations by contracting debt and issuing common shares, which is not a sustainable long-term strategy. For this reason, the market has not reacted positively. In fact, Freshpet's stock price plunged by 49.14% in the last year.

From a growth perspective, the company has achieved excellent financial results, with its revenues increasing at a 28.68% compound annual growth rate (CAGR) between 2016 and 2024. In addition, it projects an 84.58% growth in its revenues from 2024 to 2027. In the upcoming years, Freshpet also anticipates increasing its product lines from 14 to 24. This significant growth has been possible due to the considerable expenses that Freshpet has allocated to selling, general, and administrative (SG&A). To grant context, in Q1 2025, the SG&A expenses accounted for 43.79% of the company's revenues.

In 2024, the company held approximately 2.20% of the U.S. pet food market. Consequently, it will need to invest significant financial resources to become a larger player in the industry. For Freshpet, this challenge is positive and negative. On the one hand, the company has room to increase its revenues by 5–15 times in the upcoming years. On the other hand, the company could fall short and end in financial distress, since firms like Nestle have more financial resources to compete with Freshpet. When pondering all the positive and negative aspects of Freshpet, I think it is time to be patient and wait until the company demonstrates that it can generate profit.

The Company

Freshpet is a dog and cat food company headquartered in Bedminster, New Jersey, U.S. The company has one Segment, which consists of the manufacturing, marketing, and distribution of fresh dog and cat food, as well as dog treats, as depicted in the following image.

Its products are intended to be fresh and organic with minimal processing, which provides healthier food for cats and dogs. Since 89% of American families with dogs view their dogs as family members, they are more willing to spend more resources on their pets by buying refrigerated pet food, which is a premium product.

Overview

Growth is the first aspect of Freshpet, as depicted in the following chart. In 2016, its net sales were $129.71 million, and it reached $975.18 million in 2024, for a CAGR of 28.68%. This growth indicates that the company aims to gain market share. For the future, Freshpet will continue to focus on growing. In fact, it expects to reach net sales of $1,800 million in 2027, representing an 84.58% increase compared to 2024.

Investment and Growth are generally interconnected. Companies need to invest financial resources to expand their operations. Turning to Freshpet, the company has three production facilities, namely Freshpet Kitchens Bethlehem, Freshpet Kitchens South, and Freshpet Kitchens Ennis, located in Pennsylvania and Texas. These facilities have 14 production lines, and they are expected to reach more than 24 production lines in the upcoming years. The company said that it can reach $3,000 million in net sales by 2029/2030.

However, the company has an Achilles' heel. When excluding 2024, it has not reported positive net income or positive operating income since at least 2016. In the first quarter of this year, it reported a net loss of $12.70 million and an accumulated deficit of $294.50 million.

To finance its investment in capital expenditures and operations, the company has issued common shares. To provide context, Freshpet had 35,643,964 common shares in 2018, increasing to 48,777,091 common shares in Q1 2025. When there are more common shares, shareholders experience dilution in their ownership stake, as there are more shares in circulation. In addition, the company has relied on debt to finance and expand its operations. In Q1 2025, it had liabilities of $393.07 million that were related to its long-term debt.

Breaking Down Costs

To start with, Freshpet has reported considerable gross profit margins. In 2024 and Q1 2025, its gross profit margin fluctuates around 40%, as depicted in the first chart below. Consequently, the company does not report excessive costs of goods sold. However, its operating profit margin reveals that Freshpet has significant operating expenses. In fact, the company has only reported positive operating profit in 2024, as depicted in the second chart below.

So far, I have not included other companies in the analysis. The following table shows the gross profit margin of Freshpet, Nestlé S.A. (OTCPK:NSRGY), The J. M. Smucker Company (SJM), General Mills, Inc. (GIS), Chewy, Inc. (CHWY), Colgate-Palmolive Company (CL), and Post Holdings, Inc. (POST). These companies are among the leading pet food producers. The table indicates that Freshpet has a higher gross profit margin than the median of its peers. However, it has the second-lowest EBIT and EBITDA margins. Therefore, this analysis reinforces the argument that the company has high operating expenses.

SG&A expenses explain why Freshpet is unprofitable. In Q1 2025, SG&A expenses were $115.29 million, representing 43.79% of the revenues. During that period, the costs of goods sold were $159.46 million. I include the previous figure to emphasize that SG&A expenses are slightly lower than the costs of goods sold.

Selling, General, and Administrative Expenses

Since SG&A expenses are composed of several components, I think it is convenient to analyze these costs. In SG&A expenses, media costs and share-based compensation expenses have increased significantly. By media costs, Freshpet includes costs related to advertising through media outlets. To provide context, between 2022 and Q1 2025 TTM, media costs increased by 90.32%, while share-based compensation expenses grew by 122.82%, as depicted in the following chart. In that period, revenues only gained 70.42%. Media costs and share-based compensation expenses accounted for 16.89% of revenues in Q1 2025 TTM. If the company had cut these expenses by half, it would have ended the period with a positive operating profit.

Advertising in the media is the mechanism that has fueled Freshpet's revenue growth. The investment in media that Freshpet has expended is correlated to its household penetration. This correlation reaches 98%. Therefore, as the company invests more resources in media costs, its revenues increase.

It is evident that the company aims to increase its market share. However, it must reduce its SG&A expenses as a proportion of its net sales to become profitable. Pursuing only growth is not enough. I am of the opinion that Freshpet needs to generate positive earnings to become a compelling investment opportunity. So far, the company has burned financial resources.

Market Size

In 2024, Freshpet reported revenues of $975.18 million. From this total, 98% of its revenues were generated in the U.S. That year, the U.S. pet food market size was $43,450 million. Moreover, Freshpet accounted for only 2.20% of the U.S. pet food market in 2024. It will take years for Freshpet to become a leading company in the pet food industry.

I think the strategy of gaining more market share is correct. With a small market share in the pet food industry, Freshpet will struggle to compete with its larger competitors. However, the company will have to equilibrate growth and profitability.

Stock Price

The market has not been optimistic about Freshpet. Currently, its stock price is at the levels observed in February 2020, as depicted in the following chart. In fact, Freshpet's stock price has experienced a sharp decline since January of this year.

Freshpet mentions that people are hesitant to get a new dog or cat, or replace their pet when it passes away. According to the company, many Americans are not clear about the U.S. economy. Therefore, they prefer to wait until the current period of economic uncertainty disappears before bringing a new pet into their homes. Freshpet also indicates that consumer sentiment is one of the reasons that has slowed down its revenue growth rate. The consumer sentiment index is near its all-time low. This index measures the consumers' opinions about the economy and their personal finances.

Due to the environment of macroeconomic uncertainty in the U.S., the company downgraded its net sales projection for this year. In the previous guidance, it expected net sales in the range of $1,180 million to $1,210 million. The new guidance estimates revenues between $1,120 million and $1,150 million.

Common Shares and Debt

At Freshpet's current stock price, I think that the market will react negatively if it issues common shares. This year, the company's stock price has declined by 54.13%. For instance, if it intends to raise $100 million in common shares, it will have to issue more than twice the ordinary shares that it would have had to issue a year ago.

However, Freshpet still has significant liquid resources. In Q1 2025, it reported $243.73 million in cash and cash equivalents. Meanwhile, in that period, it had $395.70 million in long-term debt. In addition, in Q1 2025, it reported current assets of $ 405.39 million and total liabilities of $509.54 million. As such, I think the company has significant financial resources to sustain its operations. While it is true that the company has reported net losses between 2016 and 2023, it is also true that these losses are relatively small in comparison to its total revenue. To provide context, in 2022, the company reported $58.00 million in net losses, its worst performance from 2016 to 2024, as depicted in the following chart. At that level, the company would burn its cash and cash equivalents in 4.2 years.

Peer Analysis

This section conducts a peer analysis among Freshpet, NSRGY, SJM, GIS, CHWY, CL, and POST. These companies are relevant pet food producers. The following table shows the ratios to analyze.

In the last year, Freshpet's stock price declined by 49.14%. In addition, in a five-year period, its stock price decreased by 21.60%. Among its peers, it is the company with the worst performance in the financial market, a negative aspect.

The valuation metrics suggest that Freshpet is overvalued compared to its peers. For instance, the analyst consensus projects that the company will have a 79.28 P/E, while the median of this ratio in the peer group is 19.76. The company also has higher EV/Sales, EV/EBITDA, and price-to-cash ratios than the median in the peer group.

As commented on in the Breaking Down Costs section, the company has an excellent gross profit margin compared to its peers. However, its EBIT margin is the second lowest value due to its considerable SG&A expenses. With that being said, the company dominates all the growth metrics. For this year, the analyst consensus expects a 20.20% growth rate in Freshpet's revenues. In addition, the revenues grew at a 31.18% CAGR in a five-year period.

Finally, the company also exhibits low debt metrics compared to its peers. For instance, it has a 41.02% debt-to-equity ratio, the lowest value among its peers. Furthermore, it has a 4.91 current ratio. As such, the company has financial resources to pay its obligations.

Overall, Freshpet has better growth and debt ratios than its peers. However, the valuation ratios suggest that the company is undervalued. In addition, the company has experienced a significant decline in its stock price. Pondering all these aspects, I think Freshpet should improve its profit ratios to be an excellent investment opportunity.

Conclusion

In light of the analysis presented in this article, I think that it is advisable to wait until the company reports positive earnings. Freshpet has an excellent growth outlook compared to its peers. Its revenues grew at a 28.68% CAGR between 2016 and 2024. In addition, the revenues are expected to increase by 84.58% from 2024 to 2027. Due to this significant expected expansion, the company plans to increase its product lines to 24, from its current 14 lines.

However, the company has only been profitable in 2024. In the first quarter of 2025, it reported a net loss of $12.70 million. These losses were due to the considerable investment dedicated to expanding its operations. To provide context, in Q1 2025, Freshpet's SG&A expenses represented 43.79% of its revenues. The company dedicates significant resources to advertising.

On the other hand, Freshpet has expanded its operations by issuing shares and contracting debt. This strategy is not sustainable in the long term. I believe the company will become an attractive investment opportunity if it can generate profits. When taking all of these aspects into consideration, I think it is time to wait until the company becomes a profitable firm.

data.title: Lumine Group: The Positive Impact Of Its Smaller Size
data.description:
Introduction

In my first coverage of Lumine Group (TSXV:LMN:CA) (OTCPK:LMGIF) in late 2023, a few months after its IPO, I concluded that I favored Constellation Software (OTCPK:CNSWF) (CSU:CA) due to its track record, diversification, and higher organic growth.

While I rightfully stated that Lumine's returns on invested capital would improve with the WideOrbit acquisition, I underestimated the benefits of its smaller size, allowing it to close highly creative deals at much more attractive prices than Constellation.

Although Constellation's investors can't complain about the performance during that time, Lumine Group has delivered much better returns, driven by a 34% increase in revenues and a 55% increase in adjusted FCF per share during 2024.

Lumine Group In A Nutshell

Lumine Group acquires and manages mission-critical software companies operating in media and communications, following Constellation Software's playbook.

We follow the same hurdle rates. We have the same governance and control framework, and we operate very much like an operating group. So it's tried and tested, and it works well, and we fall in line and we support it in any case.

Source: David Nyland (Lumine Group's CEO), 2025 AGM Q&A.

The company spun out from Constellation in 2023 after its largest acquisition (WideOrbit), and currently has a market cap of $9.7B.

Since its early days as part of Volaris (one of Constellation's operating groups), Lumine has acquired 33 companies, which usually have a #1 or #2 market share in the niche where they operate.

The focus on the media and communications spaces reduces industry diversification, but Lumine seeks geographic diversification and low customer concentration, taking advantage of carve-outs and special situations such as bankruptcies to close deals at favorable prices.

Declining Businesses, But Strong Revenue Growth

Lumine's businesses, like most vertical market software companies (VMS), have clauses in their contracts to raise prices with inflation.

While the company delivered positive organic growth during 2021 amid the post-pandemic high-inflation period, the most recent quarters have shown negative organic growth.

Part of the decline has been caused by the integration and onboarding of certain acquisitions, but it could also be that Lumine's portfolio of companies has higher churn rates than its peers.

During a period where both Constellation Software and Topicus.com (TSXV:TOI:CA) (OTCPK:TOITF) delivered positive or neutral organic growth, Lumine had a -8% organic growth in 2024, and a -4% in Q1 2025.

Nonetheless, the company is delivering revenue growth above 25% (2023 was strongly impacted by the WideOrbit acquisition), from which we can infer that, on top of its ability to deploy all its FCF into acquisitions, Lumine is closing deals at attractive prices.

Recent M&A Activity

During its first year as a publicly traded company, Lumine closed 5 acquisitions, having one of its most M&A active years, especially considering the WideOrbit transaction ($490M).

In 2024, only two deals were closed, but Lumine managed to deploy $145M (80% of its adjusted FCF) at attractive prices, taking advantage of Casa Systems, Inc.'s (CASA) bankruptcy.

The most significant acquisition during 2024 was the Device and Service Management Platform businesses from Nokia (NYSE:NOK), which was initially expected to close for up to €185M (~$200M at the time), but Lumine ended up paying $110M (1.15x revenues).

The other transaction that Lumine closed during 2024 was the opportunistic acquisition of Casa System's cloud native 5G assets for $32M (0.76x revenues) following the Chapter 11 bankruptcy filing.

The 2025 acquisitions (Datafusion Systems and Vidispine) were closed during Q2, so they haven't yet impacted Lumine's financial statements, and prices have not been disclosed.

Vidispine provides management systems to media companies, and while I haven't been able to access its financials, revenues are estimated to be around $5.2M, so I don't expect a significant impact from this acquisition.

Datafusion Systems is a UAE provider of solutions used in border control and territory surveillance.

Price Paid On Acquisitions

Although Lumine hasn't disclosed the exact multiples for last year's acquisitions, we can approximate them by comparing the cash deployed with the increase in FCF.

Adjusted FCF, which I measure as cash from operations excluding change in net working capital, less D&A of tangible assets, increased by $69M in 2024 (from $113M in 2023)

When excluding the increase in share count, we get a $62M increase. In this increase, however, we also need to take into account the late 2023 acquisition of Synchronoss for $38M (0.6x revenues).

Considering the $180M spent on the three acquisitions and the $62M increase in adjusted FCF, we can approximate the price paid to ~3x FCF, in line with the 30.9% adjusted FCF margin delivered during the most recent quarter.

The low valuation multiple paid on these acquisitions may surprise some readers, but it is important to take into account that these are low-growth or even declining businesses, so Lumine needs to squeeze as much FCF as possible while they still deliver it.

Expectations: Organic Growth Will Improve

Lumine's FCF growth will be driven by its ability to deploy capital, the multiple paid on acquisitions (ROIC), and organic growth. Since it generates less than one-tenth of Constellation Software's FCF, it should benefit from its smaller size to find attractive deals.

Last year, the company improved its operating and FCF margins in every quarter (YoY), and delivered its best Q1 in 2025 with an 18.7% operating margin and 30.9% adjusted FCF margin. As intangible assets get depreciated, returns on invested capital will keep their upward trend, converging to a figure around 25% to 30% (in line with WideOrbit's ROIC).

As previously stated, organic growth hasn't performed well in recent quarters, which has been caused by short-term headwinds from the Nokia and Casa System acquisitions:

The negative organic growth during the year ended December 31, 2024 is primarily related to the carve-out acquisitions completed in Q2 2024 which are continuing to go through integration and onboarding of best practices where a reduction in organic growth can be expected as we re-evaluate contracts and projects.

Source: MD&A FY2024.

Nonetheless, the decline in organic growth improved during the last quarter, and given that WideOrbit is delivering positive organic growth, I expect an improvement in the following quarters as the most recent acquisitions are integrated.

Valuation

At the moment, Lumine is generating over $50M in adjusted FCF per quarter, and its shares are trading at 44x its expected 2025 adjusted FCF.

In the model presented below, I forecast a low but positive organic growth after 2027. If the company deploys all its capital into acquisitions, which is a fair assumption considering Lumine's track record, acquisitions should drive above 20% revenue growth.

As Lumine becomes larger, I factor in a decline in the valuation multiple to 34x LTM FCF, which provides a certain margin of safety to our assumptions.

Note: The 2025-2030 period uses a 1.37 USD/CAD exchange rate.

For 2025, given that only two acquisitions have been announced so far, and one of them will likely have a small impact, I assume a lower percentage of capital deployed compared to previous years.

Since the timing of the acquisitions will always be lumpy, I assume that over the next years, all the FCF generated will be deployed into acquisitions.

Based on these assumptions, and including the decrease in valuation, Lumine's stock would deliver a 16.8% CAGR over the next five and a half years.

Risks

Even with a decline in valuation multiples, Lumine could deliver attractive returns to its shareholders over the long term, but there are some risks to take into account.

First, the company trades at the Toronto Venture Exchange, it has a relatively low float, and lower trading volumes and liquidity. While this shouldn't bother Lumine's long-term investors, the stock can present significant volatility and it might be difficult to sell a substantial amount of shares quickly.

Lumine has an average daily trading volume of ~CAD3M, much lower than Constellation Software's 3-month average of ~CAD152M per day.

The other risk I would highlight is running out of acquisitions in the media and communications space. Although the company now has a relatively small size, the focus on a particular niche could limit its ability to deploy capital over the years.

On the positive side of covering a particular niche, Lumine is facing less competition:

In terms of copycats, yes, so yes, we haven't seen yet too many or any real significant communications and media vertical market software consolidators.

And I think it's partially because of how specialized the space is. The barriers to entry from a domain knowledge perspective are pretty high.

Source: David Nyland (Lumine Group's CEO), 2025 AGM Q&A.

Conclusion

Since the first time I reviewed Lumine, the company has demonstrated its ability to close opportunistic acquisitions at attractive prices, and I underestimated the benefits of its smaller size.

In light of the performance of the business, and despite the temporary headwinds in organic growth, I retract the title I gave to that article (Lumine Group: Current Valuation Might Not Be Justified), as I believe Lumine's premium to Constellation Software is justified.

When the most recent acquisitions are integrated, organic growth figures will improve, and returns on invested capital will continue their upward trend.

Lumine's shares could trade sideways for some months to consolidate the recent stock appreciation (unless a significant acquisition is announced), and that is the reason for my hold rating.

Nonetheless, I have a positive view on the business, and I expect it to keep outperforming due to its smaller size and higher chances of finding highly accretive deals with attractive rates of return.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Interest Rate Debate
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Biggest theme this week: increasing tension between Trump and Powell (0:25). Interesting treasury markets (4:20). Airlines earnings, sector signs (5:10). Netflix reporting; valuation concerns (7:30). Stocks continuing overall melt up (8:00).

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: Brian Stewart, welcome to a special Thursday edition of Wall Street Lunch. Thanks for joining us in Kim Khan's absence.

Brian Stewart: Always special to be here.

RS: Indeed. Talk to us about where we're at Thursday afternoon, July 17th.

BS: I think the biggest theme so far this week has been the increasing tension between Trump and Powell. Trump's gotten more belligerent about removing Powell lately. There were signs yesterday that his firing might be imminent.

However, Trump backed off from that. So we're kind of still in the limbo that we've been in for a while with Trump sniping at Powell and Powell maintaining a stony silence about the pressure that he's feeling.

It's a complicated subject. In the macro view, the most important issue is the independence of the Fed. There's a concern that if politicians start either directly fixing interest rates or have enough leverage over the Fed that they have a huge influence on the the current interest rate that will just end up with a zero interest rate in perpetuity.

It's always gonna be in a politician's best interest to have the lowest possible interest rates. That gets you low mortgage rates. It gets you low borrowing rates. So there's always gonna be political pressure to have rates super low.

Trump in fact is pushing for an extremely low rate. He's called on multiple occasions for rates to be cut by three percentage points, so down to the one percent area, which traditionally has been where things end up in dangerous emergency situations.

So it was in the 0% land for a long time after the financial crisis. It got down to 1% after the dot com bubble exploded. So you're talking about a rate that's usually only on the table for special circumstances, and that's what Trump's been pushing for lately.

RS: What else would you say about recent inflation data that we've seen? And anything to note about the bond markets, the dollar, or anything else to add to this part of the conversation?

BS: The most interesting thing about the pressure that's coming lately is it it, in a way, economically, just looking at the data, it seems to be coming out of nowhere, the idea that we would cut rates to 1%.

Again, the political pressure for that makes a lot of sense. It's the same political pressure that keeps the government cutting taxes and raising spending. So it's not that surprising in that frame.

But if you look at the economic news, it's pretty solid, 4.1% unemployment rate. That's the low end of unemployment, that's very healthy levels.

There was a contraction in terms of GDP in the first quarter, but signs so far this quarter show growth in the the mid 2% range, which is healthy where you would want it.

So there doesn't seem to be a real economic crisis bubbling up unless Trump has information that we don't have.

Meanwhile, inflation, which the core inflation rate was 2.9% in the most recent report. That's traditionally at the high end. It's disinflationary when compared to the spike that we saw in the post COVID era.

But if you look historically, 2.9% would be the high end of the range ex COVID for the twenty first century so far. So if we were sitting in 2015 with a 2.9% core CPI rate, that would be dangerously high.

And so you can understand Powell's reluctance to cut rates, especially since I think Powell probably feels burned by the post COVID situation. He left rates low for a longer time than a lot of people wanted him to and inflation got a little bit out of control.

And so I think he's very gun shy about making the same mistake again. I think looking at the the treasury markets is kind of interesting. The thirty year is currently around 5%. That's just off its highs of the year. It was 4% last September. The thirty year rate is higher than it was. It started the year around 4.8%. So it's actually gone up in 2025.

The movement in the lower duration treasuries is less substantial. The ten years around 4.4%, the five year around 4%. Both of those are down from their positions at the beginning of the year.

So you see most of the selling going on at the longer end of the yield curve. So as investors, I would keep an eye on that as this wrangling continues. I think that you're gonna see the signs of displeasure more on the longer end.

RS: Recent earnings, anything to update us on? We saw some reports out of the airlines yesterday.

BS: Yeah. United (UAL) had its earnings last night. It was choppy immediately after the report. The company hit its bottom line, beat on the bottom line, but missed on the top line.

However, the stock at last check was up about 3%. Most of the problems that it faced during the quarter were from flight disruptions in Newark. So I think investors are taking that as a sign that this was a limited situation. It was outside the company's control. And so the underlying operations remain strong.

And this follows up on a report from Delta (DAL) from last week. Delta jumps about 12% following its earnings report, and it beat expectations, set stronger than expected guidance, said that demand trends have stabilized at levels that are about flat to last year.

Meanwhile, corporate demand remains steady. So signs out of the airlines if we're framing this also in terms of the overall economic picture and the debate between Trump and Powell, the signs from the airlines are that demand remains pretty strong for air travel, which is usually a pretty good sign both of the health of the consumer and health of corporate spending.

We're gonna get more information next week. Southwest (LUV) is gonna report its earnings. The (JETS) ETF, which tracks the overall airline industry, it's up 46% since its April low. So you've seen a lot of strength in airlines.

Things got pretty rocky for the airlines earlier this year with worries about the international situation, plus the escalating violence in the Israel Iran conflict, which spiked oil briefly.

So I think that what you're seeing now is those geopolitical concerns are starting to back off a little bit, and you're seeing a relatively healthy economic situation for the airlines.

RS: Netflix (NFLX) is also reporting today after the close.

I wanted to note that we've had two recent episodes on our Investing Experts podcast. Really, really bullish thoughts and analysis on Netflix, but, serious valuation concerns.

A lot of bullishness, but not necessarily long. So if you want a deeper dive into Netflix. Anything to say about Netflix or any other stocks reporting or having recently reported?

BS: I think you summarized it really well. The signs are that Netflix has been solidifying itself as the leader in the streaming space. It's had a rebound lately, but the question becomes whether that rebound is a little overdone.

So yeah, exactly what you said. Valuation is the main concern going to the earnings now.

RS: So you wanna take us home with a brief minute or two on today's markets? And listeners, please note that tomorrow, we will be back with our regularly scheduled Wall Street Roundup. Look for that on Friday afternoon. Take it away, Brian. Thanks for joining us again.

BS: Yeah. Sure. Stocks are up mildly today, kinda just continuing the overall melt up that we've seen generally.

Major news today is the retail sales report and jobless claims. Those came in relatively benign.

So again, you're seeing a solid to strong economic situation, and I think that the debate over interest rates are going to be the the main headline getter for the rest of the month until the Fed announces its next move at the end of the month.
